cataloging_subject.label: Middle English Prose Merlin
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous mid-fifteenth-century Middle English prose translation and adaptation conventionally called the Prose Merlin. It is one intellectual work with two source-compositional sections, not thirty-three independent works. The two surviving Middle English witnesses, the Old French Vulgate Merlin and its manuscripts, Henry B. Wheatley's edition and modern English supplements, the U-M digital encoding, and the repository XML are distinct transmission or representation layers. In particular, the repository item's final printed pages 697-699 include a Wheatley translation from Old French after the principal Middle English manuscript breaks off; that terminal editorial supplement is described for representation accuracy but is not silently attributed to the anonymous Middle English translator.

catalog_summary.title: Middle English Prose Merlin
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator and adapter; first source section ultimately associated with Robert de Boron
catalog_summary.date: Near the middle of the fifteenth century; c. 1450-1460 is the conventional range printed by Wheatley
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place and dialect localization unresolved

notes: The qualifier 'Middle English' distinguishes the work from Robert de Boron's poem, the Old French Vulgate Merlin, other Merlin continuations, and modern editions.
This common short title is human-readable but can be ambiguous outside a Middle English context.

titles.value: Merlin; or, The Early History of King Arthur: A Prose Romance

scope.description: Title of Wheatley's source edition and the U-M item rather than a securely transmitted medieval title
Editorial heading at the beginning of Wheatley's text and the U-M XML

titles.value: Explicit the shutting up of Merlin. May God bring us all to a good end!

scope.description: Wheatley-edition trailer after the French-derived terminal continuation

notes: Because the principal Middle English manuscript breaks off earlier, this encoded trailer is not treated as evidence for the medieval work's original ending.

agents.name: Robert de Boron
notes: Late-twelfth-century author of the Old French verse Merlin that probably underlies the first compositional section through the prose/Vulgate tradition. This is source-work responsibility, not authorship of the surviving Middle English wording.

agents.name: Anonymous Vulgate Merlin continuator or continuators
notes: Responsible for the early-thirteenth-century Old French prose sequel incorporated into the Vulgate Cycle; the record does not assume a single identifiable author.

agents.name: Henry Benjamin Wheatley
notes: Editor of the nineteenth-century EETS source text and translator of the narrowly bounded Old French supplements used after damage, a blank, and the lost ending.

scope.description: Middle English rendering of the Old French Vulgate Merlin, excluding Wheatley's modern terminal supplement in the repository representation

notes: No personal name for the Middle English translator is securely established in the reviewed sources.

scope.description: Selection, wording, and adaptation involved in producing the Middle English prose version

notes: Conlee calls the work a straightforward and fairly accurate translation; 'adapter' preserves the modern cataloging description without implying a radically independent rewriting.

scope.description: Authorship of the late-twelfth-century Old French verse source ultimately underlying the first section

notes: The Middle English section is probably derived through a prose/Vulgate transmission rather than proved to translate Robert's surviving verse directly.

scope.description: Authorship of the Old French prose sequel source incorporated in the Vulgate Merlin

notes: This source-layer responsibility does not displace the anonymous Middle English translator.

scope.description: Transcription, chaptering, notes, and nineteenth-century EETS presentation underlying the U-M encoding

notes: This is edition-level responsibility and is not medieval work authorship.

scope.description: Modern English continuation after CUL Ff.3.11 breaks off, translated from BL Add MS 10292 and encoded at printed pp. 697-699
English repairs translated from BL Add MS 10292 for text lost through burning at CUL ff. 137-138, and one phrase supplied at a blank

notes: The repairs occur at printed pp. 387-392 and p. 650; they are not a whole-part replacement.

date_statements.display: Near the middle of the fifteenth century; conventionally c. 1450-1460
notes: Wheatley's title prints about 1450-1460, which supplies the normalized interval; current METS more cautiously says near the middle of the fifteenth century. The interval is a conventional scholarly estimate, not an exact documentary date.

date_statements.display: Fifteenth-century attestation in CUL MS Ff.3.11 and late-fifteenth-century attestation in Rawlinson MS D. 913; chronological priority is not established
notes: The broad date ranges overlap, and the checked sources do not establish which surviving witness is chronologically first.
Witness copying dates are not automatically the exact translation date.

date_statements.display: First part published in 1865; completed EETS edition issued by 1899
notes: The edition appeared in four parts; 1865 records the first publication of the work in print, not completion of the whole edition.

places.label: Arthurian Britain
notes: A legendary and literary setting, not a precise historical administrative region.

places.label: Arthurian Gaul
notes: A legendary and literary setting spanning multiple modern regions.
The reviewed sources place the Middle English work in England but do not securely localize an exact place of translation.

scope.description: Principal legendary setting, including Logres, Wales, Cornwall, Camelot, and conflicts with Saxons and rebel kings
Major campaigns and episodes involving Ban, Bohort, Claudas, Trebes, and Roman conflict

language_statements.label: Middle English

scope.description: The medieval English work through the surviving Middle English witness text

notes: Modern English and Old French in Wheatley's edition are separately scoped editorial/source layers.

language_statements.label: English

scope.description: Wheatley's English translations at printed pp. 387-392, p. 650, and pp. 697-699

notes: The classification is linguistic/editorial, not a claim that the medieval work as a whole is mixed Middle and Modern English.

scope.description: Brief French comparison readings retained in Wheatley's encoded notes; the full French transcription on print pp. 700-701 is omitted

form_statements.description: A sustained prose Arthurian romance. The Old French verse Merlin stands behind the first source-compositional section, but the cataloged Middle English work is prose throughout.

genre_statements.label: Translation and adaptation
notes: The work closely follows the Old French Vulgate Merlin; this genre statement records textual mode rather than implying known word-for-word dependence on a surviving exemplar.

subject_statements.label: Merlin
notes: The subject coverage includes Merlin's birth, prophecy, enchantment, and imprisonment.

subject_statements.label: The birth, coronation, and rise of King Arthur
Chivalry, tournaments, warfare, and military alliance
Magic, prophecy, disguise, and supernatural knowledge
The Round Table and formation of Arthurian fellowship
Preparation for the Holy Grail tradition

summaries.value: The Middle English Prose Merlin recounts Merlin's demonic conception and Christian preservation, his prophetic childhood, the fall of Vortigern, the reigns of Pendragon and Uther, Arthur's conception and coronation, and the long military and dynastic consolidation of Arthur's realm. Its Vulgate-sequel section expands into interlaced campaigns, courtly alliances, tournaments, prophecies, the formation of Arthurian fellowship, Merlin's dealings with Nimiane, and the events surrounding his imprisonment.

The work is a close Middle English prose translation/adaptation of the Old French Vulgate Merlin. Scholars distinguish an opening section ultimately associated with Robert de Boron's verse Merlin, ending with Arthur's coronation, from an anonymous early-thirteenth-century Vulgate sequel. The division is analytical and source-historical: the Middle English text merges the sections without a marked internal break.

The repository XML follows Wheatley's 33 editorial chapters and printed pages 1-699. It includes modern English text supplied by Wheatley from BL Add MS 10292 at damage/blank loci and after the Cambridge witness breaks off, but omits Wheatley's printed Old French transcription on pages 700-701. Those features belong to the edition/encoding representation and are not silently assigned to the anonymous medieval translator.

extent.description: One prose work represented in 33 Wheatley-created chapter DIV1 elements. The pinned XML has 34 HEAD elements including the body heading, 603 P elements, 699 PB elements for printed pages 1-699, 490 manuscript-folio MILESTONE elements, and 49 NOTE1 elements. The 33 chapters are representation metrics, not thirty-three intrinsic works; the two source-compositional sections are analytical, and Wheatley's terminal translation is separately modeled.

content_parts.label: Robert de Boron source section
content_parts.summary: From the council of devils and Merlin's birth through Vortigern, Pendragon, Uther, Arthur's birth, the sword in the stone, and Arthur's coronation. This section is probably derived ultimately from Robert de Boron's late-twelfth-century verse Merlin through the prose/Vulgate tradition.

locators.value: first five METS sections, through Arthur's coronation

notes: The six Wheatley chapters and five METS sections are different editorial segmentations of the same analytical source section.
No explicit medieval division marker was established at the transition.

content_parts.label: Vulgate Merlin sequel section
content_parts.summary: The long interlaced sequel after Arthur's coronation: rebellion and Saxon war, Arthur's alliances with Ban and Bohort, campaigns in Britain and Gaul, Gawain and other young knights, Arthur and Gonnore, Roman conflict, Merlin and Nimiane, Merlin's imprisonment, and the final episodes transmitted by the Cambridge witness.

locators.value: DIV1.7 through the CUL break inside DIV1.33; printed pp. 108-697; manuscript-folio milestones approximately 36a-245b
from after Arthur's coronation through the break at f. 245v/b

notes: Wheatley's translations at printed pp. 387-392 and p. 650 repair selected local defects within this section; they do not turn the whole section into a modern translation.
The final Wheatley continuation after the manuscript break is modeled separately below.

content_parts.label: Wheatley's terminal English supplement
content_parts.summary: A modern English continuation supplied after CUL Ff.3.11 breaks off, translated by Wheatley from the Old French Vulgate Merlin in BL Add MS 10292. It completes the printed and encoded reading text through p. 699 and is followed in print by the Old French source transcription on pp. 700-701, which U-M omitted.

locators.value: inside DIV1.33 after note.49; remainder of printed p. 697 through p. 699

notes: This is present in the repository representation but excluded from medieval author/translator scope.
The terminal supplement's extent is described by print pagination because the English text does not occur in a Middle English manuscript witness.

related_works.title: Vulgate Merlin

identifiers.value: Merlin section of the Lancelot-Grail or Vulgate Cycle

notes: This work-level relationship does not identify a surviving physical French exemplar used by the Middle English translator.

related_works.title: Merlin
notes: Part-scoped relationship: the opening section through Arthur's coronation is probably derived ultimately from Robert de Boron's verse poem through the prose/Vulgate tradition.

related_works.title: Vulgate Merlin sequel (historical continuation)
notes: Part-scoped relationship for the remainder after Arthur's coronation; this historical continuation is distinct from the later romantic continuation often called the Suite du Merlin.

related_works.title: Lancelot-Grail Cycle
notes: The Old French Vulgate Merlin source belongs to the larger cycle; the Middle English work is an English version of that section rather than a translation of all five major cycle components.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned source XML for Merlin, header and source description.
evidence.summary: Provides the U-M item title, Wheatley editorial responsibility, conventional 1450-1460 date in the source-edition title, 1997 encoding date, source-edition and 1969 call-number data, and selection statement.

evidence.citation: Computational inspection of General Corpus pinned source XML for Merlin.
evidence.summary: Counts one BODY, 33 DIV1 chapter elements, 34 HEAD, 603 P, 699 PB, 490 MILESTONE, 49 NOTE1, and no verse LG/L structure. Chapter pagination runs from pp. 1-699; chapter VI ends with Arthur's coronation on p. 107 and chapter VII begins p. 108.
notes: Counts describe the pinned representation and do not establish intrinsic medieval work divisions.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned source XML for Merlin, reading text and notes.
evidence.summary: Supports prose form, narrative subjects, editorial headings, Middle English wording, retained French comparison notes, and the boundary between the Cambridge text and Wheatley's terminal English continuation.

evidence.citation: Wheatley edition and repository XML evidence for passage-scoped French-derived supplements.
evidence.locator: repository note.11-note.49; print pp. 387-392, 650, 697-701; IA PDF pp. 757-761 for print pp. 697-701
evidence.summary: Confirms that burned Cambridge text was translated from BL Add MS 10292 ff. 154-155, one phrase was supplied from f. 206 col. 2, and the continuation after the Cambridge break was translated from French at f. 216; the print gives the French original on pp. 700-701, omitted by U-M.

evidence.citation: John Conlee, Introduction to Prose Merlin, Middle English Texts Series.
evidence.summary: Describes the Middle English work as an anonymous, close prose translation of the Old French Vulgate Merlin written near the middle of the fifteenth century; distinguishes the Robert de Boron and Vulgate-sequel sections; analyzes themes and narrative form; identifies the Cambridge and Rawlinson witnesses; and reports three lost final Cambridge leaves.

evidence.citation: H. L. Pink description of Cambridge University Library MS Ff.3.11.
evidence.summary: Supports the fifteenth-century date, principal-witness scope, physical extent, burned leaves, lost final leaves, paragraph-based manuscript organization, and imperfect ending.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries catalog and Digital Bodleian records for MS Rawl. D. 913.
evidence.summary: Confirms the second Middle English witness as a single-leaf fragment at fol. 43r-v within a later 129-leaf guardbook; only fol. 43r is exposed in the partial Digital Bodleian object.

evidence.citation: British Library catalog record for Add MS 10292.
evidence.summary: Identifies the 1316 Old French Lancelot-Grail manuscript and its complete L'estoire de Merlin section at ff. 76r-216v. It supports source-work context and Wheatley's scoped consultation, not a translator-exemplar claim.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Henry B. Wheatley's four-part EETS edition of Merlin.
evidence.summary: Supports Wheatley's editorial responsibility, the four-part serial publication beginning in 1865, completed 1899 presentation, EETS series context, and edition title.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan CME item Merlin and current public XML.
evidence.summary: Supports the CME/UMDL identifier, U-M title, 1997 encoding, source-edition relationship, and current public item route.

open_questions.question: Can the anonymous Middle English translator or adapter be identified more narrowly?
next_steps: Review specialist linguistic and textual scholarship for attribution proposals, preserving disputed or hypothetical names as such.

open_questions.question: What place of translation and dialect region can be supported for the Middle English work, distinct from the scribal localization of either witness?
next_steps: Consult LALME/eLALME and specialist studies of the Cambridge and Rawlinson language.
Keep authorial and scribal dialect claims separate.

open_questions.question: Can the conventional c. 1450-1460 dating be narrowed or independently justified beyond Wheatley's title and the broad current description 'near the middle of the fifteenth century'?
next_steps: Review codicological, paleographical, and linguistic dating studies.
Do not equate witness copying date with composition date without argument.

open_questions.question: Which Old French Vulgate Merlin manuscript or textual family supplied the Middle English translator's exemplar?
next_steps: Consult specialist textual comparisons across the Vulgate Merlin tradition.
Treat BL Add MS 10292 only as Wheatley's passage-scoped editorial source until direct translator-exemplar evidence is found.

open_questions.question: Can the Robert de Boron/Vulgate-sequel transition be located more precisely in every witness and edition without treating later section or chapter counts as intrinsic?
next_steps: Compare Conlee's first-five-section boundary, Wheatley's chapter VI ending at printed p. 107, and the corresponding manuscript folio sequence in a full facsimile.

open_questions.question: Did the principal Cambridge codex lose two or three leaves at the end?
next_steps: Reconcile Wheatley's two-leaf statement with Pink's and current METS's three-leaf collation using current CUL codicological evidence.

notes: The 33 XML chapters are Wheatley's editorial organization. Conlee's current METS edition uses 25 sections. Neither number is promoted to an intrinsic medieval-work item count.
The Cambridge witness is the only continuous Middle English copy, not the only surviving Middle English material: Rawl. D. 913 preserves a short independent fragment.
The analytical two-section model concerns source composition. The third content part in this manifest is a repository-representation supplement introduced by Wheatley after manuscript loss.
BL Add MS 10292 is evidence for the Old French source tradition and for Wheatley's explicit supplements; it is not asserted to be the Middle English translator's physical exemplar.
Dates, places, languages, rights, and access claims belong to their documented layers and should not be flattened across work, witness, edition, encoding, and facsimile.
